name: A Goblin's First Scrap
summary: The Goblin Elder wants to see if you can fight. Find him in the settlement to begin.
intro: Ah — a fresh goblin, still smelling of the spawning pit! Good. Before you're any use, you must learn to scrap. Head EAST to the training yard and batter one of the rats. Click the ground to walk, click a rat to hit it.
outro: Ha! Not bad for a first swing. You'll do. Here — a few coins to start you off, and my blessing. The settlement is yours to explore; folk here have work for a goblin with initiative. Look for the '!' markers.

steps.say: Click the ground to send Gork walking. The yard is just east of me.
There's the rat. Left-click it to attack — Gork will walk up and swing on his own.

steps.text: Pick up the Bones it dropped
steps.say: It dropped some bones. Walk over them to pick them up — that's how you loot.

steps.name: Goblin Elder
steps.say: Now bring those bones back to me, back west in the settlement.

name: First Pickaxe
summary: The General Store Keeper needs copper from the quarry.
intro: You've got arms on you — how about you use 'em? There's copper up in the Grubpit Quarry, NORTH of town. Take a pickaxe, bring me five copper ore. Simple.
outro: Proper ore, that. Knew you had it in you. Here's your cut — and remember, anything you gather, you can sell. Try the Grand Exchange by the fountain.

steps.text: Travel north to the Grubpit Quarry
steps.say: Head north up the road. Watch for cave bugs — you can fight or run past them.
Click the copper rocks with a pickaxe equipped or in your pack.

steps.name: General Store Keeper
steps.text: Bring the ore back to the Store Keeper
steps.say: Good haul — now carry it back to me in the settlement.

name: Fish for the Chief
summary: The Wharf Fishmonger is short on shrimp for the Chief's supper.
intro: Chief wants shrimp and the Chief being hungry is EVERYONE'S problem, understand? Grab a small net and pull three shrimp from the water here on the Wharf. Go on.
outro: That'll keep the Chief off our backs. Cook 'em on a fire or range and they'll even heal you in a fight. Here's your pay, little fisher.

steps.say: Click the fishing spots along the Wharf with a small net to catch shrimp.

steps.name: Fishmonger
steps.text: Hand the shrimp to the Fishmonger
steps.say: Bring those shrimp back to me here on the Wharf.

name: Goblin Needs Pointy Stick
summary: The Weapon Shop Keeper in the Forge Ward thinks you need a real blade.
intro: That twig you're waving? Embarrassing. A proper goblin carries a Bronze Dagger. Buy one from me, smith one at the anvil, or take one off something — I don't care how. Just come back armed.
outro: NOW you look like a warrior. Bronze today, iron tomorrow. Keep at the smithing and you'll out-fight the lot of them. Here — a little something for listening.

steps.say: Buy one from my shop, smith one from a bronze bar at the anvil, or loot one.

steps.name: Weapon Shop Keeper
steps.text: Show the dagger to the Weapon Keeper
steps.say: Bring it here and let me see it, back in the Forge Ward.

name: Cabbage for Cowards
summary: The Greengate Grocer swears cabbage makes a goblin brave.
intro: Cabbage! Cabbage makes a goblin BRAVE — that's science, that is. Get down to the farmlands SOUTH of the gate and bring me back three good heads.
outro: Look at these beauties. You're braver already, I can tell. Don't argue. Take your coins and eat your greens.

steps.say: Follow the south road out of Greengate to the farm plots.
Pick cabbages from the farm patches.

steps.name: Grocer
steps.text: Bring the cabbage to the Grocer
steps.say: Carry them back to my stall at Greengate.

name: Rats in the Storehouse
summary: The General Store Keeper has a rat problem — and needs a goblin who can fight.
intro: Right, you can swing a blade now — prove it. My storehouse is overrun. Clear out FIVE of the rats in the training yard and I'll make it worth your while. Off you go.
outro: Five rats, gone. You're handy in a scrap, I'll give you that. Here's your pay — and if you want a real challenge, the world beyond the gates is full of it.

steps.say: The yard's east of town — thin them out.

steps.name: General Store Keeper
steps.text: Report back to the Store Keeper
steps.say: Come tell me when the storehouse is clear.

name: The Hollow Idol
summary: The Goblin Elder has felt something stir beneath the old training yard. He needs a brave goblin to face it.
intro: You've proven you can fight. Good — because I need you for something the others are too frightened for. There is an old idol beneath the training yard, older than the warren itself. Lately it… whispers. Take it three bone offerings, and whatever crawls out — put it down.
outro: So. The Hollow Idol is silent at last, and you walk away wearing its crown. The whispers named you, goblin — I heard them. Keep the Fang close. I suspect this was only the beginning.

introCutscene.say: You hear it too, don't you? The hum beneath the yard, every night since you arrived.
Go. Silence the idol before it wakes the whole warren.

outroCutscene.say: …at last… a goblin worthy of the crown…

steps.text: Seek the idol beneath the old training yard
steps.say: The Elder points east, past the training posts. The ground hums as you approach.
Bones for the idol — the rats in the yard will provide. Lay three at its feet.
The idol splits open with a crack of old stone — and something climbs out!

steps.name: Goblin Elder
steps.say: It's done. Carry the crown back to the Elder in the settlement.

name: Bridge Over Dumb Water
summary: The Timber Row Foreman wants the West Bridge rebuilt — and a squatter cleared off it.
intro: That river's been cutting us off from the willow lands for a season, and now some bandit's set up on the old crossing. Bring me timber and nails, then go clear the bridge yourself. Ten logs, five oak, three bronze nails.
outro: A bridge AND one less bandit. The willow lands are open again — good work, goblin. This is more than pocket change; you've earned it.

steps.say: Chop the trees around town with a hatchet.
Oak grows in Chopper's Hollow to the west — you'll need Woodcutting 15 for it.
Hammer nails from a bronze bar at the anvil.
The crossing is west of town, at the river's edge.
There's the squatter. Send him packing.

steps.name: Timber Row Foreman
steps.say: Come tell me the bridge is ours again.

name: The Grubpit Problem
summary: The Miner Camp Boss can't work the quarry — it's crawling with bugs.
intro: Can't get a lick of work done in that quarry — cave bugs everywhere and imps nicking the ore. You look like you can swing. Clear 'em out: eight bugs, two imps. Then the copper's ours again.
outro: Quarry's quiet for the first time in weeks. The lads can mine again thanks to you. Take this — miners look after those who look after them.

steps.say: The quarry's in the hills — head there and get to work.
Squash the bugs infesting the tunnels.
The imps are tougher — mind yourself.

steps.name: Miner Camp Boss
steps.text: Report to the Miner Camp Boss
steps.say: Come back to the camp when the quarry's clear.

name: A Proper Goblin Axe
summary: The Weapon Shop Keeper dares you to forge your own iron battle-axe.
intro: Bronze was for the fledgling you were. Time for iron. But I'll not hand it to you — SMELT three iron bars and FORGE the battle-axe yourself. That's the only way it means anything.
outro: You made that. With your own hands and your own fire. That's a real goblin's weapon. Wear it proud — you've earned every swing.

steps.say: Iron ore plus the furnace, at Smithing 15. Watch for failed smelts early on.
Take the bars to the anvil and hammer out the axe.

steps.name: Weapon Shop Keeper
steps.text: Show the axe to the Weapon Keeper
steps.say: Bring it here so I can admire your handiwork.

name: The Farmer's Bad Smell
summary: Something is rotten in the farmlands — pests, and a smell nobody will name.
intro: Crops are crawling with bug swarms and there's a stink over the whole field I won't describe to you. Clear four swarms and bring me five onions — Grocer swears the onions help with the smell. They don't. Do it anyway.
outro: Fields are clearing and I've onions to spare. The smell? Still there. But the crops'll live, and that's down to you. Here's your wage, pest-hunter.

steps.say: Back down the south road to the plots.
The swarms move fast — corner them among the rows.
Pull onions from the farm patches while you're there.

steps.name: Farmhand
steps.text: Bring it all back to the Farmhand
steps.say: Carry the onions up to the shed at Greengate.

name: Grublake Fish Thieves
summary: Someone is raiding the Grublake nets. The Fishmonger wants them caught.
intro: Been losing fish off the Grublake nets every night — crazed fisher-goblins, gone feral. Head EAST to the lake, catch six of 'em in the act, and land three honest trout to prove the water's still good.
outro: Six thieves and a fine string of trout. The nets are safe and the Chief eats well. You've come a long way from netting shrimp on my Wharf, goblin. Well earned.

steps.say: Take the east road past the Oakwoods to the lake.
They lurk by the water — deal with them.
Fish the lake spots for trout while you're here.

steps.name: Fishmonger
steps.say: Bring the trout back to my Wharf.

name: Sparks of Invention
summary: Sprocket the Tinker, near the settlement, reckons a goblin with initiative could learn to build machines that go BANG.
intro: Oi! You've got clever hands, I can tell. Tinkering's the goblin art — bombs, cannons, contraptions. But first you scavenge. See that Rough Scrap Heap? Claw 5 bits of scrap out of it (no tools needed) and bring 'em back.
outro: HA! Look at that — proper scrap. You're a Tinker now, near enough. Here's a Rusty Wrench so you can crack open the tougher heaps, and use my Workbench any time. Come back when you want to learn about POWDER.

steps.text: Salvage 5 Scrap Metal from a Rough Scrap Heap
steps.say: Rough Scrap Heaps are near the settlement and the Old Forest Ruins. Click one — bare hands work fine.

steps.text: Bring the scrap back to Sprocket
steps.say: Got 5? Bring 'em here.

name: Powder and Patience
summary: Sprocket wants the makings of blackpowder: saltpeter from the rocks and charcoal from the fire.
intro: Now — BANG needs powder, and powder needs saltpeter and charcoal. Mine me 3 Saltpeter (Quarry or Mine Hills), and char 3 logs into Charcoal at my Workbench. Then we'll talk.
outro: Beautiful. Mix saltpeter, sulfur and charcoal and you've got blackpowder — the heart of every good bomb. Bombs and the Bombard are yours to build now. Go make some noise.

steps.say: Saltpeter Deposits sit in the Grubpit Quarry and Northern Mine Hills. Needs a pickaxe.
Open the Workbench and 'char' some logs into charcoal.
Powder's within reach — come tell me.

name: A Bigger Bang
summary: Prove you can handle powder, and Sprocket will teach you Hand Cannons.
intro: Powder's fine, but a Tinker dreams of CANNONS. Build me 3 Blackpowder and forge an Iron Barrel, and I'll show you how to point a hand cannon at something you don't like.
outro: There it is — the Hand Cannon line is yours. Slugs punch clean through armour. No knight stands a chance against a goblin with a tube of powder.

steps.say: Saltpeter + Sulfur + Charcoal at the Workbench.
Two Iron Bars at the Workbench.
Bring the powder and barrel here.

name: Tools of the Trade
summary: The master's lesson: build tools that do YOUR work for you.
intro: Last lesson, and the best one: a real Tinker builds machines that make the OTHER skills easier. Build a Hand Cannon and blast 3 foes with it, then I'll teach you powered tools — a Prospector's Lens, a Powered Pickaxe, the lot.
outro: Now you're a proper Tinker. Powered tools speed your mining and chopping, and the Lens sniffs out richer veins. Go build an empire of gadgets, goblin.

steps.say: You'll need casings, an iron barrel, a grip and a trigger assembly.

steps.text: Defeat 3 Training Rats with your gadget
steps.say: Equip the cannon + slugs and blast three Training Rats (east of spawn).
Come collect your reward.

name: Scouts in the Oakwoods
summary: The Goblin Elder has had word: rival scouts are creeping through the Eastern Oakwoods.
intro: War is coming, and it starts in the trees. Rival goblins are scouting the Eastern Oakwoods to the FAR EAST — six of them, at least. Cut them down, and skin a couple of the moss wolves while you're at it; we'll want the pelts. Go.
outro: Six scouts silenced and good pelts besides. The Red-Ears will think twice about the oakwoods now. Wear the Mosswarden Cape with pride — the trees chose you.

steps.text: Travel east to the Eastern Oakwoods
steps.say: Take the east road past Grublake, deep into the oaks.
They travel in ones and twos — hunt them through the trees.
The moss wolves prowl the same woods. Two good pelts will do.

steps.name: Goblin Elder
steps.say: Bring the pelts and the news back to me in the settlement.

name: The Bog Bites Back
summary: Something in the Bog of Grub is stirring, and the Elder wants it understood before it's too late.
intro: The scouts were the easy part. The Bog of Grub to the SOUTHEAST has gone wrong — the slimes are thick as soup and the water's turned black. Clear five of the bog slimes and bring me three swamp herbs. Tread careful. The bog remembers.
outro: Black water, black herbs, and something older than all of us waking beneath it. You did well to come back at all. Rest — because I fear the worst is still down there.

steps.text: Journey to the Bog of Grub
steps.say: Follow the south road past the farms, then east into the mire.
They split and reform — hit them hard and fast.
The herbs grow where the water's blackest.

steps.name: Goblin Elder
steps.text: Report back to the Goblin Elder
steps.say: Bring the herbs and what you've seen back to me.

name: Heart of the Bog
summary: The thing beneath the Bog of Grub has a name now. The Elder asks you to end it before it fully wakes.
intro: It has a name, the thing in the bog. Mggwrath. The Sunken One. It slept a thousand years and your war woke it. There is no army for this, goblin — only you. Go to the black heart of the bog and put it back to sleep. Forever.
outro: The bog is still. The water runs clear for the first time in living memory, and you walk out of it carrying the Sunken One's own barbed spear. The warren will sing of this. …Rest, hero. You've more than earned it.

introCutscene.say: Whatever you do down there — do not let it speak your name three times.

outroCutscene.say: …you… will… drooown… like all the… rest…

where.name: The Black Heart of the Bog

steps.text: Descend to the black heart of the bog
steps.say: The water goes still as glass. Something vast turns beneath it.
The bog erupts — a mountain of black water and teeth rises to meet you!

steps.name: Goblin Elder
steps.text: Return to the Goblin Elder, a hero
steps.say: It's over. Carry the spear — and the story — back to the Elder.

name: The Great Snail Derby
summary: Murk the bogling means to win the Great Snail Derby a tenth time. His champion is off his feed and the racetrack's thick with biters.
intro: The Derby's near! Nine years running I've taken the ribbon, and I mean ten. But my Slugworth's off his feed and the run's crawling with skeeters. Fatten him on bog-beans, swat the biters off the track, and we ride to glory together.
outro: TEN years! Slugworth crossed a clean snail-length ahead — the rival keepers are FURIOUS, which is the sweetest part of winning. Here. A bogling counts you a friend now, and out here in the muck that is no small thing.

steps.text: Travel to the Bog of Grub
steps.say: South and east, past the willows, where the ground gives way to muck.
A champion won't race hungry. Bog-beans grow thick round the pools.

steps.text: Clear 6 Bog Mosquitoes off the racetrack
steps.say: The skeeters pester the snails something awful. Swat the lot.

steps.name: Murk the Bogling
steps.text: Return to Murk for race day
steps.say: Bring the beans back and we'll line up at the start.

name: What the Pumps Drain
summary: The old dwarf pumps still turn out in the deep bog, draining water to nowhere anyone has followed. Murk never cared to know why. You might.
intro: The pumps? Iron things, big as huts, out east in the deep muck — dwarf-work, older than boglings, older than the bog maybe. They pull the water down and down and no one's fool enough to follow the pipes. But you've got that look. Go on, then. Mind the horrors that nest in the warm iron.
outro: So they still turn. So the water still goes... wherever the water goes. You see? Nobody follows the pipes and comes back the wiser — the old iron keeps its own counsel, friend, and maybe that's for the best. Come, there's rust to bless and a snail to feed.

steps.text: Find the dwarf pumps in the deep bog
steps.say: East, where the ground steams. You'll hear them turning before you see them.

steps.text: Clear 3 Bog Horrors nesting in the warm iron
steps.say: They love the heat of the old machines. Mind their reach.

steps.text: Pull 4 Mudroot choking the pump intake
steps.say: The roots clog the intake. Clear them and watch the water run down.

steps.name: Murk the Bogling
steps.say: Come tell me what you found out there. Or what you didn’t.

name: The Great Cap Festival
summary: The Great Cap Festival is the fashion event of the year — and the spore-crawlers have chewed Old Judge Morel's centerpiece caps to lace with the show hours away.
intro: Disaster, darling, DISASTER — the crawlers have gnawed my centerpiece to ruin and the Festival is UPON us! Forty years I've judged in this very hat and I shan't see the show spoilt. Bring me the reddest redcaps and one witchcap for drama, then clear those wretches from the show-grove. The finest heads in the realm are counting on us.
outro: Sublime. SUBLIME. The redcaps caught the light and the witchcap drew audible gasps — the Red-Ear delegation actually applauded, the brutes. You've a moss-folk's eye for a cap, friend, and out here that is the highest praise there is. Wear this one with pride.

steps.say: Far to the southwest, where the canopy glows. Follow the spores in.
The reddest you can find — presentation is EVERYTHING.
One perfect witchcap. It simply must have PRESENCE.
Shoo the little wretches before they lace another cap.

steps.name: Old Judge Morel
steps.say: Bring the caps to the festival pavilion — quickly, quickly!

name: A Stranger's Afternoon
summary: Now that you've earned a moss-folk's trust, Old Judge Morel shares the secret of the mooncaps: eat one and dream a stranger's whole afternoon. Whose, no one has ever learned.
intro: You've earned a moss-folk's trust, so I'll tell you what we don't tell outsiders. The big pale mooncaps — eat one and you'll live an afternoon that isn't yours. A stranger's lunch, their quarrel, the face of someone they loved. We've dreamed these dreams for generations and never once learned whose lives they are. Gather three ripe mooncaps and dream for yourself. Just... don't go looking too hard for the who of it. That way lies a kind of madness.
outro: So. You dreamed it — the crooked little kitchen, the argument about the leaking roof, the child's laugh you'd swear you knew. And you've no more notion whose life it was than I do, than my grandmother did. That's the way of the mooncaps, friend: some afternoons belong to no one we'll ever name. Best we leave them their peace.

steps.say: Deeper in, where the caps glow palest. Mind the crawlers underfoot.

steps.text: Drive off 5 Fungal Rats gnawing the mooncaps
steps.say: The rats reach the ripe ones first. Shoo them off.

steps.text: Gather 3 ripe Mooncaps to dream by
steps.say: Pale and heavy — those are the ripe ones. Three should do.

steps.name: Old Judge Morel
steps.say: Come back when you've dreamed. Tell me what you saw. Or don't.

name: What the Sprites Tend
summary: The forest-sprites keep the old ruin-chapel swept and lit, though they won't say why. Thistle needs a hand — the ruin-wisps have grown bold and scattered the chapel's records.
intro: Oh — a visitor, and a big one. We keep the chapel, we sprites. Sweep it, light it, mind the stone. Don't ask us why; that's not for telling. But you could HELP, couldn't you — the ruin-wisps have gone bold, blown out our lanterns and carried off scraps of the old chapel-map. Drive them from the nave and bring back what they stole, and we'll call you a friend of the ruin.
outro: Lanterns lit, map-scraps home, wisps seen off — the chapel's proper again. You've a gentle hand for a goblin, and the ruin knows it now. Come back when you can; there's always tending to do. And perhaps, one day, I'll tell you a little of the why.

steps.text: Travel to the Old Forest Ruins
steps.say: North and west, where the forest swallows old stone. Mind the guardians.
They blow out the lanterns and scatter our things. Chase them from the nave.
The wisps carried off pieces of our old map. Bring them back to me.

steps.name: Thistle the Sprite
steps.say: Bring the scraps to the chapel door and we’ll set things right.

name: Sized for Giants
summary: The ruin doors are cut too clean and sized too tall — for something bigger than a troll. Thistle won't say who for, but says fresh orders in an unreadable hand still appear when the tending's done. Someone still expects this place kept.
intro: You want to know the why, don't you. I said I might tell you... but the truth is we don't KNOW, not truly. We tend because we've always tended. Only — there are notes. Orders, in a hand none of us can read, that appear when the work is done. Someone still expects this place KEPT. The guardians hold the key to the deep vault where the last of them lie. Get past them, take the key, and see for yourself. Then... let it lie.
outro: So. Fresh ink on stone older than the forest, in a hand sized for a hand far bigger than yours or mine — an order to keep the ruins kept, signed by no name we can read. Someone still writes them. Someone still expects. Who? We don't know, friend — not the sprites, not the trolls, not the oldest oak. And I'll tell you true: the ones who go looking for the who don't come back the same. Let it lie. Help us tend, and let it lie.

steps.say: Past the chapel, down where the guardians walk. Bring a light.
They wake for intruders. Put them back to their sleep.

steps.text: Take the rusted key to the deep vault
steps.say: A guardian keeps the vault key. Prise it loose and open the deep door.

steps.name: Thistle the Sprite
steps.text: Bring what you found to Thistle
steps.say: Come tell me what the order said. Or that you’ve chosen not to.

name: Trouble in the East Adit
summary: The hauling crews won't go down the east adit — mine-skulkers have moved in and the ore carts come up light. The Camp Boss wants his shaft back.
intro: You picked a fine day. My haulers won't set boot in the east adit — skulkers have moved in, thieving ore straight off the carts, and every cart that does come up comes up light. Clear the wretches out and haul me up a proper load of iron so the crews see it's safe. And listen — whatever you do down there, DON'T whistle. Camp rule. Don't ask.
outro: Carts running full and the crews grumbling about honest work again — that's the sweetest sound in these hills. You did fair work down there, goblin. Take the belt; a skulker won't be needing it. And you didn't whistle, did you. Good. Don't ask.

steps.text: Travel to the Northern Mine Hills
steps.say: North road, past the farmlands, up where the headframes stand.

steps.text: Clear 6 Mine-skulkers from the east adit
steps.say: They nest in the dark past the third lamp. Root them out.

steps.text: Haul up 4 Iron Ore to prove the shaft safe
steps.say: A full load up the ramp and the crews will follow.

steps.name: Miner Camp Boss
steps.text: Report back to the Camp Boss
steps.say: Come tell me the adit’s clear and the carts are rolling.

name: The Straight Dark
summary: Every goblin shaft eventually breaks into an older, straighter dwarf tunnel going further down than anyone has dared. Cairn the Pebbler wants to listen to it — through the fangs of the bats that roost where the whistling starts.
intro: ...Sit. ...No — I'll speak quicker, for you. The goblin shafts wander. The old tunnels don't. Straight, and down, and further down than daring goes. ...I would LISTEN to what walks there. The cave-bats roost where the whistling starts; their fangs hold the echoes they fly through. Bring me two. The crawlers that nest where shaft meets tunnel will mind — un-mind them. ...Go. I will still be here.
outro: ...Hm. ...Yes. The machines, still turning. Water, going down. And under it — steps. Far down. Slow. ...Unhurried, the way stone is unhurried. ...You want to know who. I know the beginning of it. I began telling it forty years ago and I am nearly through the second word. Sit, if you like. ...Or come back. The answer isn't going anywhere. Neither, friend, is what's down there.

steps.text: Descend to where the goblin shaft meets the dwarf tunnel
steps.say: Down past the last lamp, where the walls go straight and the picks go quiet.
They nest at the meeting of the ways. They will mind you. Un-mind them.

steps.text: Collect 2 Echo Fangs from the cave-bats
steps.say: The bats roost where the whistling starts. Their fangs keep what they fly through.

steps.name: Cairn the Pebbler
steps.text: Bring the fangs back to Cairn
steps.say: ...I will be where you left me. Pebblers usually are.

name: Ask the Wood
summary: Old Taproot the root-keeper will teach a goblin to take from the Oakwoods properly: ask first, use all of it, thank after. The boars are goring saplings faster than the wood can grow them — a cull the oaks themselves have asked for.
intro: You came through here once with a blade, chasing Red-Ears. The wood remembers — no, don't look like that; it remembers KINDLY. But if you'd take from the Oakwoods, you'll take our way: ask first, use all of it, thank after. The boars are goring saplings faster than the wood can grow them back — that's a cull the oaks have asked for themselves. Bring me the meat; nothing asked-for goes to waste. And gather oak while you're out. The trees will show you which.
outro: Meat for the table, oak for the beam, and the saplings breathing again. That is taking done properly — the wood ends heavier by what you left and lighter by what you carried. You goblins usually have that backwards. Come deeper into the wood sometime. There's someone I tend that you ought to meet. Bring patience.

steps.say: East, past the lake road, in under the old canopy.

steps.text: Cull 6 Oak Boars goring the saplings
steps.say: The wood has asked. Make it quick and make it count.

steps.text: Bring back 4 Boar Meat — nothing goes to waste
steps.say: What is asked for is used, all of it. That is the bargain.

steps.text: Gather 3 Oak Logs the asked way
steps.say: Take from the trees that offer. You will know them when you look.

steps.name: Old Taproot
steps.say: Come back to my porch — mind the roots, they move a little.

name: The Oldest Oak
summary: In the deepest Oakwoods stands the tree the root-keepers have tended since before remembering — and lately, black root-things gnaw where its oldest roots run. Taproot won't say how old it is. Taproot doesn't know.
intro: This is the one I tend. Older than the Ridge trolls, older than the ruins — how much older I won't say, partly because we don't say, and partly, friend, because I don't KNOW. My grandmother tended it. Hers did. Somewhere down the years someone must have asked it, but the answer isn't in the family anymore. Only the tending is. Now — black root-things stalk its floor, gnawing where the oldest roots run. Clear them off and bring me the blackroot they shed. The oak won't thank you aloud. It never does. But watch for a leaf or two coming down where you stand. That's it, saying.
outro: Blackroot burned, floor cleared — and its leaves came down for you; I saw. You want to ask it, don't you. How old. What it's seen. Whether it grew the first of us, or we planted it. I asked once, when I was green — it dropped an acorn on my head. Sixty years I've thought about that answer, and here's where I've landed: some things aren't secrets. They're just too big for the door of a word. Let it be old. Let it be kind. That's enough to be going on with.

steps.say: Deepest part of the wood, where the canopy goes quiet. You will know it. Everyone does.

steps.text: Destroy 3 Blackroot Stalkers gnawing the roots
steps.say: They gnaw where the oldest roots run. The oak cannot ask them to stop. You can.
Bring me what they leave behind. It burns hot, and it deserves to.

steps.name: Old Taproot
steps.text: Return to Old Taproot beneath the oak
steps.say: I will be under the old one, watching the leaves.

name: Best in Fair
summary: The harvest fair is days away, scavengers are raiding Granny Furrow's prize plot, and the trophy — which is named after her — is NOT leaving the family.
intro: Fair's in three days, my war pumpkin's the finest thing I've grown in forty seasons, and the scavengers have found my plot. The trophy is named AFTER me, child. It is not leaving the family to a pack of thieving vermin. Run them off, and while you're out, pull me a proper show of cabbages — the judges dock you for a thin table, and my table has never once been thin.
outro: Look at that pumpkin. LOOK at it. The judges wept, the young ones sulked, and the trophy stays where it belongs — that's the fair run honest, which is to say, won by me. You worked for the table, so the table provides: take this one off my own vine. Mind how you carry it. It's won things.

steps.say: South of town, where the crop rows run to the lake.

steps.text: Run off 6 Farm Scavengers raiding the prize plot
steps.say: Thieving vermin. Make the lesson stick.

steps.text: Pull 5 Cabbages for the fair table
steps.say: A thin table loses points. My table is never thin.

steps.name: Granny Furrow
steps.say: Back to my plot, and wipe your boots.

name: Ten Paces West
summary: The scarecrow in the far field has moved again — ten paces west, and the Farmhand MEASURED. Nobody works that field anymore. Somebody should at least go look at it.
intro: It moved. Again. Ten paces west — I MEASURED, don't you give me that look. Nobody works the far field anymore; nobody but me so much as walks it. Go out there and look at it. Stand with it a while. And take it three cabbages — it liked the cabbages, last year. ...Take turnips too, in case its taste has turned. Don't laugh. DON'T laugh.
outro: Still where you found it? 'Course it was. It never moves while somebody's watching — ten years, never once while watching. That's not the part that gets me, friend. The offerings. Were they gone when you looked back? ...They always are. A fox, you'll say. Fine. A fox. ...It's facing my house now. Did you notice that? It didn't used to face my house.

steps.text: Walk out to the far field
steps.say: Past the last worked rows, where the fence wants mending. You will see it. It has seen you.
It liked the cabbages last year. Set them down gentle.

steps.text: Bring 3 Turnips, in case its taste has turned
steps.say: Ten years is long. Tastes turn.

steps.name: Farmhand
steps.say: Come find me at the shed. I will want to know exactly where it stood.
